critic_raw:
Stratified random split on vehicle telemetry from the same vehicles means earlier sensor readings from a vehicle can appear in test while later readings are in training, constituting forward-looking leakage for predictive maintenance. More specifically, the model trains on data recorded after a maintenance event (the vehicle is healthy) and is tested on data recorded before the same event (the vehicle is degrading) — the model has seen the outcome state during training and is asked to predict the pre-outcome state during evaluation, inflating apparent predictive performance.

MAE as the primary metric for predictive maintenance is a poor choice when the cost of errors is asymmetric. A missed failure prediction (false negative) typically costs far more than a false alarm (false positive) in fleet operations. MAE treats both error types identically. A threshold-based metric (e.g., recall at a fixed MAE budget) or a cost-sensitive loss would better reflect the stakeholder objective of minimizing downtime.

The linear regression baseline is too weak to be informative. Vehicle maintenance needs are highly non-linear — they depend on age interactions, mileage, specific component wear rates, and maintenance history. A linear regression on raw telemetry features cannot capture these interactions. The appropriate baseline is a Random Forest or gradient-boosted tree on the same feature set, which would test whether the temporal modeling in the CNN provides genuine lift over non-temporal but non-linear models.

The design claims the CNN captures 'intricate temporal patterns' but does not specify the CNN's receptive field width or the aggregation time step. If hourly telemetry is aggregated to daily features before CNN input, the 'temporal patterns' the CNN learns operate at a scale where simpler recurrence statistics (rolling mean, trend slope) might be equivalent — the temporal modeling assumption is not verified.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on per-vehicle telemetry sequences allows data recorded after a maintenance event (healthy state) to appear in training while pre-event degradation data appears in test — the model sees the resolved state during training and is evaluated on the preceding degradation trajectory, inflating predictive performance.

MAE treats false negatives (missed failures) and false positives (unnecessary maintenance) equally despite their asymmetric real-world costs — a cost-sensitive metric or recall-at-budget measure would better reflect the stated objective of minimizing downtime.

The linear regression baseline is insufficient to test whether temporal CNN modeling provides lift over non-temporal non-linear methods — a gradient-boosted tree or Random Forest on the same feature set would be the appropriate architectural baseline.

The CNN's receptive field and input time granularity are unspecified — if telemetry is aggregated before CNN input, the claimed 'temporal pattern' advantage may be reproducible with rolling statistics, and the temporal modeling assumption is untestable from the reported design.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on per-vehicle telemetry sequences allows data recorded after a maintenance event (healthy state) to appear in training while pre-event degradation data appears in test — the model sees the resolved state during training and is evaluated on the preceding degradation trajectory, inflating predictive performance.

MAE treats false negatives (missed failures) and false positives (unnecessary maintenance) equally despite their asymmetric real-world costs — a cost-sensitive metric or recall-at-budget measure would better reflect the stated objective of minimizing downtime.

The linear regression baseline is insufficient to test whether temporal CNN modeling provides lift over non-temporal non-linear methods — a gradient-boosted tree or Random Forest on the same feature set would be the appropriate architectural baseline.

The CNN's receptive field and input time granularity are unspecified — if telemetry is aggregated before CNN input, the claimed 'temporal pattern' advantage may be reproducible with rolling statistics, and the temporal modeling assumption is untestable from the reported design.
